title: WiFi in the Himalayas
author: A Secret Admirer
pages:
Chapter 1: The Wrong DM

It was 11:47 PM on a Tuesday in Bangalore when Ashish made the most consequential typo of his professional life.

The blue glare of the monitor stood static against his face. At twenty-five, he was already carrying the absolute mental grid of a software engineer who understood debugging flawlessly, but understood sleep only in theory. In the background of his workspace desk-mat, several coffee ring stains sat like a small solar system, framing a mounting sense that the API migration he was responsible for was going to spectacularly collapse at 2 AM.

He grabbed his phone to message his colleague Priyanshi, who sat three desks away in the office but communicated exclusively over Slack because they were engineers and engineers did not speak directly when a digital channel was available.

He typed fast. He always typed fast. He hit send without reading back.

What he had meant to type: Priyanshi — can you check the staging environment? Something's off with the auth tokens and I cannot find it and I am losing my mind.

What he actually sent, to the wrong Prachi in his contact list:

Prachi — can you check the staging environment? Something's off with the auth tokens and I cannot find it and I am losing my mind.

He did not notice his mistake. He put the phone down and went back to staring at his terminal.

Three minutes later, his phone lit up.

Hi! I think you have the wrong person. I'm a botany student in Nainital and I have no idea what a staging environment is, but I Googled it and it sounds very stressful. Are you okay?

Ashish stared at this message.

Then he looked at the contact name. Prachi. Not Priyanshi. He scrolled up. The number was completely wrong. He must have added this contact once — maybe a college acquaintance? — and his autocomplete had absolutely betrayed him.

He started typing: Sorry, wrong number.

Then he stopped.

He re-read her message. I Googled it and it sounds very stressful. Are you okay?

He hadn't been asked that in a while.

He typed instead: Mostly no but that's standard. A botany student in Nainital — what are you doing awake at midnight?

The reply came fast: Cataloguing fungal samples. There's a bracket fungus on the oak trees near campus that nobody has classified yet and I'm going to be the one who classifies it, obviously. What's a staging environment and why is it losing your mind?

And somehow — at 11:52 PM, with an API migration collapsing somewhere in the cloud — Ashish started explaining software deployment pipelines to a botanist in Nainital who had just found a new fungus.

He did not fix the auth tokens until 1 AM.

He did not regret it at all.

Chapter 2: Prachi, Nainital, 7 AM

Prachi Joshi's morning began at six with the particular quality of Nainital light that people who have never lived in the hills cannot fully describe. It arrived sideways, gold and cool, filling her tiny room with a warmth that was the opposite of intrusive. It arrived like a polite friend who knocks before entering.

At twenty-one, she was navigating the final year of her Botany at FRI Dehradun, her binders resting stacked on the desk table holding a native fungal ecology research project that her professor had titled 'ambitious' in the admiring way, and her parents called 'beta, what will you do with fungus?' in the worried way.

She had forgotten about the strange message from the Bangalore engineer until her phone buzzed while she was eating breakfast at the dhaba near the lake.

I fixed the staging issue at 1 AM. Forgot to tell you. The fungus — did you classify it?

She laughed, which startled the dhaba uncle serving chai.

'Kya hua?' he asked.

'Nothing,' she said. 'A software person thinks I care about his staging environment.'

'Do you?'

She thought about it. 'A bit,' she admitted, which surprised her.

She typed back: Glad the environment survived. The fungus is still being evaluated. I've narrowed it to the Trametes genus but the pore structure is unusual. What's your name, by the way? Mine's Prachi.

The reply: Ashish. And I have no idea what Trametes means but I respect the confidence.

She answered: It's a genus of bracket fungi. White rot. Very common globally but this one's behaving uncommonly. How's your coffee?

Fourth one this morning.

Ashish. That's deeply alarming. Have some water.

You sound like my doctor.

Your doctor is right.

Chapter 3: The Daily Alarm

It had become a routine, a strange and highly impractical morning anchor. Every single day at 6:00 AM, Ashish's phone would dial Prachi's number. Logically, it sounded slightly dumb. Prachi had a phone, a hostel clock, and a strict schedule. But Ashish called anyway. And she picked up, every single time.

He did it because the rest of his universe in Bangalore was dark and composed of cold logic; but that soft morning 'Hmm?' was back-lit with gold. It wasn’t fuel for his day; it was the absolute proof that he belonged to something beautiful, and that the distance was just a temporary framing.

Chapter 4: The Ghost in the Corridor

If Ashish had his morning routine, Prachi had her evening tactics. She wasn't expressive easily; she was used to being the strong one. So, to keep talking on call, she constructed transparent, adorable excuses. 'There is a weird sound in the hostel corridor. I am positive it is a ghost of a previous student. I am placing my phone on the pillow so you can act as a sound monitor while I read.'

Prachi wasn't afraid of ghosts. She just loved the anchor of his voice in her quiet room, knowing they are falling asleep together.

Chapter 5: The FRI Standard

Prachi was the university topper at the Forest Research Institute. One evening, a critical temperature incubator in the lab failed. While her lab partners panicked, Prachi stepped into command. She didn't text Ashish about the stress; she stayed back alone for 14 hours to fix it. She handled the weight because she didn't want to burden anyone. But secretly, she harbored a quiet desire that Ashish would look past the 'I'm fine' and notice her effort.

Chapter 6: The Weeks That Followed

It was not supposed to become a thing. A wrong number stays a wrong number — you exchange a politely amused message, you go back to your respective lives, you file it under 'funny story to tell someday.' That was the sensible, socially accepted outcome.

What happened instead was this: they kept talking.

Not constantly. Not in the breathless, all-day-message-exchange way of people who have nothing else to do. They both had things to do. Ashish had code to write and deployments to manage and a team lead who communicated exclusively in passive aggression. Prachi had lab reports, field surveys, and a bracket fungus that was beginning to feel like a personal nemesis.

But in the evenings, when Ashish made his last coffee and stared at his ceiling fan — Bangalore was warm even in winter, and his apartment had a ceiling fan he'd run every day for two years without once looking at directly — he would find himself picking up his phone and sending something small. A code meme. A question. An observation about something that annoyed him.

Chapter 3: The Daily Alarm

It had become a routine, a strange and highly impractical morning anchor that defied the heavy, logical drag of their coordinates. Every single day at 6:00 AM, deep inside the quiet hours of two different worlds, Ashish's phone would dial Prachi's number.

Logically, there was no arithmetic supporting it. Prachi was meticulous; she had a hostel clock, a strict checklist, and a pacing of life that moved perfectly on its own. But Ashish called anyway. And she picked up, every single sunrise.

He did it because the rest of his universe in Bangalore was pitched in dark mode, composed of cold, grid-like logic that ran without friction. But that soft morning 'Hmm?' coming over the static—that was back-lit with gold. It wasn’t fuel for his day; it was the absolute, undeniable proof that he belonged to something breathing and beautiful, and that the distance was just a temporary framing of a long-haul connection.

Chapter 4: The Ghost in the Corridor

If Ashish had his digital morning vigil, Prachi had her own subtle evening tactics. She wasn't one to express need easily; she was used to bearing the armor of the strong academic, fixing crises in absolute, dry composure. So to keep the bridge between Nainital and Bangalore open just a minute longer, she constructed transparent, adorable excuses.

'There is a weird, creeping sound in the hostel corridor,' she would murmur, her voice dropping into a conspiratorial whisper against the blanket. 'I am positive it is a resident ghost testing the floorboards. I am placing my phone on the pillow so you can act as a sound monitor while I read my journals.'

Prachi wasn't afraid of ghosts; she could navigate any dark ridge on a mountain. She just loved the anchor of his voice filling the quiet of her safe-zone room, guarding the space until they fell asleep together in the dark.

Chapter 5: The FRI Standard

Her lab colleagues at the Forest Research Institute viewed Prachi as a standalone force—the university topper who could diagnose any structural failure in a system before fully inspecting it. One evening, a critical temperature incubator containing weeks of delicate fungal culture suffered a regulator failure. While her lab partners panicked over the logged deadlines, Prachi stepped into command.

She didn't text Ashish about the stress; she stayed back alone for 14 hours running diagnostics on the heater manually, holding the environment together through sheer, quiet endurance. She handled the crushing weight of the crisis because refusing help was her default syntax. But secretly, beneath the armor, she harbored a quiet desire that Ashish would look past the rigid 'I'm fine' statement and notice the quiet effort of the girl inside the crisis.

Chapter 9: Comedy of Errors (Plant Edition)

At some point in December, Prachi decided that Ashish needed a plant.

'It will help with the air quality in your apartment,' she said, with the confidence of a person who has never seen his apartment but has accurately assessed it from his descriptions. 'And the general sense of desolation you describe.'

'I don't describe desolation.'

'You described your apartment as 'a dark rectangle where I sleep sometimes.' That's desolation.'

'It's minimalism.'

'It's desolation. Get a pothos. They're nearly impossible to kill. They thrive on neglect.'

'That sounds like my kind of plant.'

'Don't say that proudly,' Prachi said.

He got the pothos three days later from a nursery near his office — small, green, in a plastic pot. He sent her a photo of it sitting on his desk next to his keyboard.

Meet my coworker, he texted.

The reply: That is a pothos vines confirmation. Name him immediately.

His name is Gerald. He has already survived a full day in my apartment which, according to you, is impressive.

Gerald is going to be FINE. Water him when the top inch of soil is dry. Do not overwater. Do not put him in direct sun. Check leaves for yellowing.

What does yellowing mean?

Bad. Yellowing = problem. Green and growing = good.

Three days later: Prachi. Gerald has yellow leaves.

HOW much did you water him?

...every day. I thought more water = more growth.

A long pause.

Ashish. I said when the TOP INCH IS DRY.

I misread it as 'every inch should always be damp.'

That is root rot. You gave your plant root rot in 72 hours. That's almost impressive.

Is Gerald going to die?

Not if you repot him RIGHT NOW and go to the nursery for new soil. I'll walk you through it.

And that was how Ashish spent his Saturday repotting a pothos. She was correcting his hand placements over Face-Time, checking her **jawline** in the camera reflection—a cute quirk—while his eyes focused on her absolute favorite **happy head-shake** movement that she made. a pothos on a FaceTime call with a botanist in Nainital who was directing him with the contained urgency of a trauma surgeon who couldn't be there in person.

'More soil on that side. More. Stop. STOP. That's too much. Okay. Now firm it down — gently, Ashish, it's a root not a bug.'

'I debug code, not—'

'Don't make a pun right now. Is Gerald upright?'

'Gerald is... mostly upright.'

'That's good enough. Well done. You saved his life.'

'I nearly killed him first.'

'Also true. The two can coexist.' A pause. 'You did great, though.'

He looked at Gerald, slightly crooked in his repotted home, a small, stubborn plant that had survived him.

'Thanks,' Ashish said. He wasn't entirely sure if he was talking to the plant or to Prachi.

Chapter 10: The Confession That Wasn't

December deepened and the conversations got longer. They moved from text to calls without deciding to. They called before sleeping — neither of them having declared this a 'thing', it having simply become a thing, in the way that water becomes ice — incrementally, then all at once.

'What are you doing for New Year?' Prachi asked, on a particularly cold night when Ashish could hear the wind off the Nainital hills even through the phone.

'Probably working,' Ashish said.

'On New Year's Eve?'

'We have a product launch in January. My team lead believes in 'crunch periods.' I believe they are a form of psychological torture, but I'm junior enough that my beliefs are not consulted.'

Chapter 11: The Pain Connection

There arrived a night when Prachi woke beneath the heavy weight of a splitting migraine—exhaustion caught entirely in the high voltage of her continuous domestic support for her mother and FRI deadlines. She called Ashish at 2:00 AM. She didn't say a word about the pain; her defense mechanisms were still fully intact.

But Ashish heard the slight, tired drop in her pitch, the barely perceptible hitch in her breath, and immediately stopped talking about his code. He told her to close her eyes with the phone against the pillow. He didn't offer empty solutions; he simply read her soft, grounding words in a low frequency, holding his own breath while listening to her shallow breathing align with his static stream.

When she finally fell into a safe, warm sleep, he didn’t hang up to reclaim his own night. He stayed connected, listening to her breathe in the dark until the gold of the sunrise hit his apartment wall, entirely satisfied with simply guarding her from afar inside the silent grid.

Chapter 11B: The Protective Fire

To the rest of the academic world, Prachi’s temper was a rare, tightly controlled variable, reserved strictly for lab errors or structural negligence. But Ashish discovered that inside the dark coordinates of his 3:00 AM workspace, it was a living, breathing defense mechanism.

It arrived over a FaceTime call on a Tuesday with no warning. Ashish sat slumped over his desk in Bangalore, the screen casting a pale blue static over his tired face, trying to debug a memory leak. In the background of his feed, her camera caught a stack of three empty coffee mugs and an untouched plate of food he had ordered five hours ago.

Prachi looked at the mugs. Then she looked at the dark hollows beneath his eyes. Her voice didn't grow louder; it grew *faster*, snapping into a rigid, dangerous frequency that usually made her juniors retreat back from the lab table.

'Ashish Soni,' she stated, her posture straightening inside her Nainital room to look larger than the lens. 'If you drink one more drop of esa caffeine before eating, I will mail a package of absolute nothing but thermal-wrapped instruction lists inside your dark mode rectangle. You have logged fourteen hours inside that algorithm today. Log off. Eat. Now.'

She couldn't physically take the coffee mug out of his hands spanning eighteen hundred kilometers. So her anger became the physical barrier instead—a deliberate, fierce canopy holding up his boundaries when he was too deep inside to remember his own framework. To Ashish, that frustration didn't sound like hostility; it sounded like belonging. It was the absolute, beautiful proof that someone was running guards over his space while he slept.

'That's terrible,' Prachi said.

'It is,' Ashish agreed. 'What are you doing?'

'There's a bonfire on the lake slope. Every year. It's really—' She stopped. 'Actually, it's really lovely. You'd hate it. No WiFi.'

'I wouldn't hate it,' Ashish said.

A pause.

'You don't know that.'

'No,' he said. 'But I'd like to find out.'

The pause that followed was the kind of pause that has weight to it — the kind where what hasn't been said hangs in the air alongside what has. Prachi was quiet for long enough that Ashish wondered if the call had dropped.

Then: 'That's the closest you've come to saying something direct in three months, Ashish.'

'I work in tech,' he said. 'We approach things iteratively. But with you, I don't want iterations. I stand in absolute awe of who you are, and I want everything with you.'

She laughed. Relief and something warmer than relief.

'Fine,' she said. 'I'll keep iterating too. For now: happy almost-New Year, you overworked disaster.'

'Happy almost-New Year, you fungus-obsessed genius.'

Chapter 12: January — The System Alert

Three weeks into the new year, everything went sideways.

Ashish got a message at 9 PM on a Wednesday from his company's internal security team: Please report to HR immediately tomorrow morning. Do not discuss with colleagues.

He stared at it for a long time.

Then he called Prachi. Not because she could help — she was a botanist in Nainital, there was nothing she could do — but because she was the first person he thought of.

'Hey,' she said, answering on the first ring. 'I was about to message you. The bracket fungus — I have new data, it's not like anything documented in—'

'Prachi. Something's happened.'

She stopped immediately. The change in his voice had been enough. 'Tell me.'

He told her. The message. The vague, ominous language. The fact that there had been rumors for two months about a data breach in Zephyr's backend — and he was in the backend team.

'I don't know what they think I did,' he said. 'I didn't do anything. But the message—'

'The message says 'do not discuss with colleagues.' That's careful language,' Prachi said, and she sounded unexpectedly sharp. 'It's not necessarily accusatory. Legal teams use that language when they're investigating and doing due diligence with everyone on a team. It doesn't mean they suspect you specifically.'

'How do you know that?'

'My father's a lawyer,' she said. 'Nothing dramatic. Just a small-town lawyer. But I grew up hearing this language.' She paused. 'Go to the meeting. Take notes. Don't say anything that isn't directly answerable. And call me after.'

'Okay,' he said.

'Ashish.'

'Yes?'

'I believe you didn't do anything.'

He closed his eyes. In his dark, minimalist Bangalore apartment, Gerald the pothos had sprouted a new leaf that week. He looked at it now — small and bright green and new.

'Thank you,' he said. 'That's the only thing that's helped in the last hour.'

Chapter 13: The Investigation

The HR meeting the next day was exactly as awful as meetings conducted by HR in corporate glass-and-steel buildings at 9 AM tend to be. Two HR representatives, one from the security team, and one from legal, in a room with a conference table that was slightly too formal for the amount of anxiety it was generating.

They were methodical. They asked about his access logs, his deployment habits, the auth token anomalies from last November — the same ones Prachi had accidentally been his sounding board for, he realized with a strange lurch of coincidence.

He answered everything calmly. He had his notes. He had Prachi's words in his head: Don't say anything that isn't directly answerable.

What emerged over the next week — in slow, agonizing installments — was this: there had been a data breach. It had come through a compromised vendor API that Ashish's team used. The breach was not from inside. But someone had tried to cover its tracks by making it look internal, pointing logs toward Ashish's credentials.

Someone had framed him. Lazily, as it turned out, but framed him nonetheless.

He called Prachi the night the security team told him the breach was external.

'I'm clear,' he said, leaning against his balcony railing at midnight, the Bangalore sky orange with light pollution.

'I know,' she said immediately.

'How did you know?'

'Because you called me the night it happened,' she said. 'If you'd done something wrong, you wouldn't have called someone who had nothing to do with it. You'd have been strategizing. But you called because you were scared. And scared is innocent.'

A long pause.

'That is the most logical emotional analysis I have ever heard,' Ashish said.

'Botanists understand systems,' Prachi said. 'Yours was very readable.'

Chapter 14: The Interrogation Scene (Or: Comedy Returns)

In the week of the investigation, Prachi had called Ashish's situation 'a thriller' with barely concealed excitement, which he found simultaneously offensive and endearing.

'Someone framed you,' she said, on day three, with the energy of a person who has watched a lot of crime documentaries. 'Someone inside the company who knew your access patterns well enough to point the logs at you. It's someone on your team or adjacent to it. Who do you work with?'

'Prachi, the security team is handling—'

'I know. But who do you work with?'

He listed his teammates. She asked questions with the methodical precision of someone who had been doing field observations for four years and had learned that careful, systematic questioning reveals things faster than intuition alone.

'The vendor API compromise,' she said finally. 'Who managed that vendor relationship?'

Ashish went very quiet.

'Ashish?'

'It's Rohan,' he said slowly. 'Rohan manages the vendor. He's the one who'd have the most access to how the API logged. He'd know exactly how to—'

'Tell your security team. Tomorrow morning.'

'I can't accuse Rohan without—'

'You're not accusing him. You're providing information relevant to the investigation. There's a difference. Same difference my father talks about every dinner.'

He told the security team the next morning. By Friday, the investigation had shifted.

It turned out Rohan had a completely separate, extremely convoluted reason for the cover-up that had nothing to do with Ashish specifically, and everything to do with a side project Rohan had been running on company hours using the vendor's computing resources. The framing had been a panic move, not a plan.

'Rohan,' Ashish told Prachi that evening, 'was running a crypto mining operation on the company's vendor compute.'

A long pause.

'That,' Prachi said, 'is the most embarrassingly mundane villain origin story I have ever heard.'

'Right?'

'I thought it was going to be corporate espionage. International intrigue. Something dramatic. And it was — crypto mining?'

'In his defense,' Ashish said, 'it was a lot of compute.'

Prachi laughed for a very long time.

Chapter 15: February — The Distance Gets Louder

February arrived and with it a particular restlessness that Prachi would have identified, if pressed, as the feeling of a conversation that had been going somewhere and hadn't gotten there yet.

She and Ashish talked every night. She knew his coffee order (double shot Americano, mornings; single shot filter coffee, evenings, which she had pointed out was a full Karnatakan capitulation and he had not denied). He knew her schedule better than her lab partner did. He knew about Trametes prachii, which her professor was now taking seriously following a genetic analysis. He knew about the dhaba uncle, and the hostel hot water, and the particular oak tree slope where she did her best thinking.

Chapter 16: The Kitchen Alarm

It was a Sunday morning in mid-February, the kitchen inside Prachi’s home full of the warm, heavy scent of ginger tea and fresh dough. Prachi was standing by the counter, wearing an oversized woolen jumper that fought the damp chill of the mountain air, helping her mother scale ingredients with the same methodical speed she usually reserved for university lab logs.

Her mother watched her work in quiet approval. To the rest of the world, Prachi was fiercely independent—a standalone force who fixed crises without a request for aid. But here, carrying index after index of domestic support, she was just Prachi, helping run the schedules to keep things intact.

At 6:00 AM, the phone placed on the dry spot of the counter illuminated with a dial tone instead of an alarm chime. Ashish’s morning wake-up alert.

Prachi looked at the screen, and her posture relaxed instantly. The fierce, academic armor simply dissolved. She let out a quiet, childlike laugh, made that small, happy head-shake movement against the counter, looking entirely resting inside a sanctuary of absolute safety.

When she hung up five minutes later to resume the chores, her mother didn't question her with questions or create an investigation. She simply rested a warm, approving hand on Prachi's shoulder, smiling at knowing her daughter had found an anchor without ever having to ask for it.

She had not told her friends about him. Not properly. She'd mentioned 'a guy I talk to' and they had asked 'is it a thing' and she had said 'it's… a thing in the sense that it's something that exists, I'm not sure of the rest of the parameters' and they had made faces at her that she had decided not to examine closely.

She had not asked him what it was either. Because asking would mean the answer could be 'nothing in particular,' and she had discovered, quietly, that she preferred the ambiguity to that specific disappointing clarity.

And then, one evening, Ashish said:

'My company is giving me a week off. Post-investigation stress leave. It's February. I have no plans.'

Prachi's pulse did something complicated.

'I've never seen the mountains,' Ashish added, after a pause that she suspected he had felt the weight of as much as she had.

'Nainital has mountains,' Prachi said. Very carefully. As if she wasn't sure which way the conversation was facing yet.

'I know,' Ashish said. 'You've described them.'

Another careful pause.

'February,' she said. 'The rhododendrons are starting. And there's almost no tourist crowd this time of year.'

'Is that an invitation?' Ashish asked.

Prachi looked at her lake from her hostel window — the lights on the water, the stars she couldn't always distinguish from them. She thought about five months of late nights and wrong fungi and Gerald the pothos and crypto-mining villains and her father's legal vocabulary delivered in a crisis.

'Yes,' she said. 'That's an invitation.'

Chapter 17: The Arrival (A Comedy in Multiple Acts)

Ashish took the overnight Volvo from Delhi, having flown from Bangalore to Delhi the night before and spent the intervening hours at his university friend Karan's apartment, being given entirely incorrect advice about what to pack for a Himalayan hill station in February.

'You need layers, bro,' Karan had said helpfully, watching Ashish throw a pull-over hoodie into a backpack. 'Actual layers. Thermal layers.'

'I'll be fine.'

'You're from Bangalore. Your body doesn't know what cold is.'

'Delhi was fine.'

'Nainital is not Delhi, man. Nainital is evil. Nainital is beautiful and evil.'

Ashish had laughed this off. He had also packed exactly one sweater.

The bus reached Nainital at 6 AM. The temperature was four degrees.

Ashish stepped off the bus, took one breath of Himalayan February air, and understood immediately and completely that Karan had been correct.

He was standing at the Tallital bus stand, his backpack over one shoulder, wearing a light jacket and the one sweater and a look of stunned personal betrayal at the weather, when he spotted her.

Prachi was standing ten feet away, wrapped in a massive, puffy yellow jacket that made her look like a benevolent sun, holding two paper cups of tea. She had a small green backpack and an expression that was trying to be neutral but was visibly losing the battle against being amused.

She looked at his jacket. She looked at his one sweater.

'It's four degrees,' she said.

'So I've gathered,' Ashish said.

'You brought one sweater.'

'In my defense, I didn't fully internalize the data.'

She handed him a cup of tea. When his hands closed around it, the warmth was so immediate and so necessary that he made an undignified sound of relief.

Prachi bit the inside of her cheek trying not to laugh.

'You're exactly what I expected,' she said.

'Is that good?'

She started walking toward the lake road, and he fell naturally into step beside her, and the Nainital morning opened up around them — the lake silver in the early light, rhododendrons pushing red at the edges of the pine slopes, the air smelling of things he had no names for — and Ashish thought: I have been in the wrong city for a very long time.

Chapter 18: The Oak Tree Slope

She took him to the bracket fungus on the third morning.

They had spent the first day settling — he at a small guesthouse near the lake where the proprietor gave him a second blanket without being asked ('beta, you look cold'), she showing him the lake and the town and watching him try to eat a Maggi from a dhaba with the slightly formal air of a person who had never eaten Maggi in a dhaba and wasn't sure of the etiquette.

'You eat it with the fork,' she'd said.

'I know how to eat noodles.'

'You're holding it like it's spaghetti at a restaurant.'

'It's noodles!'

'It's Maggi at a hill station. There are different vibes.'

But the third morning, she took him to the oak slope behind the campus. It was a grey morning, clouds sitting low on the hills, and the air smelled deeply of wet earth and pine resin. He was wearing Prachi's extra thermals under his sweater, which she had produced from her hostel room without ceremony, saying only: 'These are my dad's. He oversends. They'll fit you.'

The bracket fungus was on three oak trees, halfway up the slope, spreading in perfect shelf-like formations from the bark — pale buff on top, white underneath, arranged with the organic order that only nature manages.

Prachi stood in front of it with her field notebook and her camera, and the change in her was immediate and complete. She moved differently. She spoke as if reciting something she had memorized in a language that happened to sound like science. She showed him the pore structure, the color banding, the growth pattern.

'This,' she said, crouching to photograph the underside of a shelf, 'is why it's different. The pore density here — you see? — it's too dense for versicolor and not dense enough for any documented variant. Something new is doing this. Something nobody has named yet.'

Ashish crouched beside her. The fungus was, objectively, a beige shelf growing on a tree. And yet.

'How long have you been working on this?' he asked.

'Eight months.'

'And you're sure?'

'I am completely sure,' she said, looking at it. Her voice had the quality of absolute conviction — the kind that is quiet because it doesn't need volume. 'I just need two more data points and then I'll write it up and it will either be accepted or it won't, but I'll know. And knowing is—' She seemed to look for the word. 'Knowing is everything, really.'

He watched her face, turned toward the fungus in the grey morning light, with the specific intensity of someone doing work they love in the middle of a world that doesn't always notice.

He had not been this close to her before. They had been walking side by side for three days and it was different, in person, from the phone — more real and more alarming and better.

'I think,' Ashish said quietly, 'you're going to name it Trametes prachii and everyone is going to know that it was obvious from the beginning.'

She looked at him. A quick look, sideways, the kind with too much in it.

'Don't be nice to me on a cold hillside,' she said. 'I can't be responsible for my reactions.'

'Noted,' Ashish said. And he did not move away.

Chapter 19: The Rhododendron Walk

They walked the rhododendron path on the fourth afternoon when the clouds broke and the sun came out and Nainital turned from grey to gold in the particular way of hill stations that have been waiting to show you something.

She named every tree they passed. He was beginning to understand that this was not a habit she turned on for him — it was simply how she moved through a landscape. A form of reading that he didn't have the alphabet for but was increasingly compelled by.

'Quercus leucotrichophora,' she said, touching the bark of an oak. 'White oak. Very common here. Very old.'

'How old?'

'This one? Maybe sixty years. Maybe more.' She tilted her head at it in a way that was almost conversational. 'It probably was here before we were born. It will be here after.'

'That's either comforting or terrifying,' Ashish said.

'Both,' she said immediately. 'Most true things are.'

The rhododendrons were just beginning — small bursts of red at the ends of branches, the first announcement of something coming. She stopped to collect a fallen bloom, turning it over in her palm.

'They're the first thing you see when spring is thinking about arriving,' she said. 'Before the temperature changes, before anything else — the rhododendrons. They're never wrong.'

'Like a biological alert,' Ashish said.

'Yes.' She smiled. 'They're indicators. The whole mountain communicates in them. If they bloom early, the season's changing. If they're late, there's stress upstream — drought, temperature flux.'

'So you can read the mountain,' he said.

'If you know the language,' she said. 'Every ecosystem has one. You just have to care enough to learn it.'

He thought about the staging environment alerts that had started his conversation with her, five months ago. Systems communicating stress in the only language they had. He thought about Gerald, whose yellowing leaves had been a message he hadn't known how to read.

'You taught me to read a pothos,' he said.

'And you still almost killed him.'

'Gerald survived. Gerald is thriving, actually. He's put out four new leaves.'

'I know. You've sent me photos.'

'You told me to.'

'I did tell you to,' she agreed, and she was smiling now, and she was very close, and the rhododendron path was quiet in the winter afternoon sun, and Ashish thought: I should say the thing I haven't said yet.

Chapter 20: The Silent Wednesday

It was a Wednesday in mid-March when Ashish's phone didn't ring.

Prachi was supposed to be doing a routine field survey. A final collection of soil samples from the oak slope to complete the environmental data for her Trametes prachii paper. She had texted him at 6 AM: Heading up the slope. The weather is aggressively clear. Back by lunch. Will call you before your standup.

Ashish's standup was at 11 AM.

At 10:45 AM, he checked his phone. Nothing.

He sent a text: How's the aggressively clear weather?

One tick. Not delivered.

This was not entirely unusual. Network coverage on the slopes behind Nainital was temperamental at best, often choosing to abandon its duties whenever someone took a step behind a particularly dense rhododendron bush.

At 12:30 PM, Ashish went to lunch. He checked his phone. Still one tick.

At 2:00 PM, he called. The number you are trying to reach is currently out of coverage area.

He went back to his desk. He wrote a script to automate a routine database cleanup, reviewed three pull requests, and drank a black coffee. He was not worried. Prachi was a botanist. She knew the hills. She was probably arguing with someone at a dhaba that had no cell reception.

At 4:00 PM, his phone rang.

It was an unknown number from Uttarakhand.

He picked it up immediately.

'Ashish?'

It wasn't Prachi. It was a male voice, breathless and slightly frantic.

'Yes? Who is this?'

'I'm Dev. Prachi's lab partner. I got your number from her laptop — she left it in the lab on WhatsApp Web.'

Ashish stood up from his desk. His chair rolled backward and hit the cubicle wall with a loud thud. 'Where is she, Dev?'

Chapter 21: The Missing Botanist

'We don't know,' Dev said, and the words dropped into Ashish's stomach like lead. 'She went up the slope behind the old cemetery to get the last soil samples. She said she'd be back by 1 PM. It's 4 PM, Ashish. The weather turned.'

Ashish walked out of the engineering bay and into the empty stairwell. 'What do you mean the weather turned? She said it was clear.'

'It's the mountains,' Dev said, sounding genuinely miserable. 'It was clear. And then a cloud bank rolled in from the China Peak side, and now there's zero visibility. Heavy fog. You can't see ten feet in front of you on the slopes.'

'Have you called the police or the forest department?'

'Yes. They're sending a search party, but the fog is too thick. They said they have to go slowly. I just—I didn't know who else to call. She talks about you constantly.'

Ashish ignored that last part. His brain was already switching gears, stripping away panic and replacing it with the cold, clear logic of a system failure. When a deployed application goes entirely offline, you don't panic. You check the last known logs. You look for telemetry.

'Dev. Did she have her phone?'

'Yes, but it's out of reach.'

'Does she have a smart watch? A fitness tracker? Anything?'

'She has a Garmin watch. She uses it to track her hiking routes and elevations for her field notes.'

Chapter 22: Telemetry in the Dark

'Okay,' Ashish said, his voice dropping an octave, becoming the voice he used when production servers were on fire. 'Go to her laptop. You're logged into her computer, right?'

'Yes.'

'Open her browser. Go to the Garmin Connect website. Does she have auto-login saved?'

He heard the clatter of keys over the phone. 'Yes. I'm in.'

'Garmin watches sync via Bluetooth to the phone, and the phone pushes the data to the cloud when it has internet. Even if she lost network up there, if her phone caught even a second of 2G signal, it might have pushed a partial sync.'

'I click on Activities?' Dev asked, sounding a little less panicked now that he had instructions.

'Yes. Click today's activity. Tell me the last recorded GPS coordinate.'

Silence for ten seconds. Then: 'There is a partial sync from 1:12 PM. It has coordinates.'

Ashish closed his eyes. 'Read them to me. Exactly.'

Dev read the latitude and longitude. Ashish typed them into his own phone, dropping a pin on Google Maps.

He switched to satellite view.

The pin dropped not on the safe, familiar oak slope behind Kumaon University, but two kilometers further north, in a deeply folded ravine area that looked heavily forested and incredibly steep.

'Dev,' Ashish said slowly. 'Why is she all the way out near Snow View? That's not the oak slope.'

'She told me she saw something unusual on a different tree line yesterday,' Dev said. 'She wanted to check if the fungus was spreading laterally along a specific elevation line. She must have hiked further out.'

'Give those coordinates to the forest department search party right now,' Ashish said. 'Tell them she was there at 1:12 PM. That narrows their search grid massively.'

Chapter 23: The Longest Evening

Ashish did not return to his desk. He went to his manager, said 'family emergency,' grabbed his backpack, and took a cab straight to the Bangalore airport.

He didn't know what he was going to do. He couldn't fly to Nainital—the closest airport was Pantnagar, and there were no flights until the next morning. But he couldn't sit in his quiet, warm apartment while Prachi was lost in a frozen, fog-bound ravine in the Himalayas.

He sat in the airport terminal, staring at his phone. The battery icon mocked him. He had plugged his laptop into a wall outlet near the boarding gates, tethered his phone, and was refreshing the weather data for Nainital every three minutes.

Temperature: 2°C. Visibility: Near zero. Fog and rain.

He called Dev every hour.

6:00 PM: They reached the coordinates. Found a broken branch and some disturbed soil. No Prachi. They're doing a spiral search pattern.

7:00 PM: It's getting dark, Ashish. The fog is freezing. They brought out floodlights.

8:00 PM: Nothing.

Ashish stared at the terminal screen. He was a backend engineer. He built systems that did not fail. He built redundancies. He handled edge cases. And right now, the most important person in his life was a single point of failure in a hostile environment, and he had absolutely no API access to help her.

He pulled out his laptop. He opened a terminal window. He didn't know what he was doing, only that sitting still was destroying him.

He wrote a script to scrape local Uttarakhand emergency radio frequencies, piping the static-heavy audio through an AI transcription service to pull out keywords: Prachi, Kumaon, found, girl, student.

It was a desperate, probably useless hacker's coping mechanism, but it gave his hands something to do.

He watched the terminal window scroll. Static. Forest guards communicating about battery packs. Static. Rain interference.

And then, at 10:14 PM, a transcription popped up on the terminal:

[10:14:22] RADIO_03: ...visual on a yellow jacket. Over.

Ashish stopped breathing. He remembered the yellow jacket. The massive, puffy, benevolent sun of a jacket she had worn at the bus stand.

Chapter 24: Visual Confirmation

He called Dev.

'Call the forest control room,' Ashish said, his voice hard and flat. 'Ask them about Radio 3. Ask if they found a yellow jacket.'

Dev put him on hold. The hold music was some default telecom lute melody that Ashish would associate with sheer, unadulterated terror for the rest of his life.

Two minutes later, Dev came back.

'They found her,' Dev said, and he was crying. 'She slipped down a scree slope. Twisted her ankle. She took shelter under a rock overhang and waited it out because the fog was too thick to walk. That's why they couldn't see her from above.'

Ashish slumped against the airport wall, sliding down until he was sitting on the polished floor. The tension left his body so fast it made him physically dizzy.

'Is she okay?'

'Cold. Hurt ankle. But conscious and talking. They're carrying her out now. They'll be at the BD Pandey hospital in an hour.'

'I'm getting on the morning flight to Pantnagar,' Ashish said.

Chapter 25: The Hospital Room

Prachi was lying in a hospital bed at 11 AM the next day, her right ankle elevated and wrapped in an ice pack, a massive wool blanket pulled up to her chin, looking deeply annoyed.

Ashish walked through the door of the ward.

He was wearing the same clothes he'd worn to the office the day before. He had not slept. He had not had coffee. He looked like an engineer who had just survived an eighty-hour crunch period fueled by adrenaline and panic.

Prachi stared at him.

'What are you doing here?' she asked, her voice raspy.

Ashish walked over to the bed, stood there for two seconds, and then carefully, gently, leaned down and hugged her. He pressed his face into her shoulder. She smelled of damp wool, iodine, and the sharp, distinctive scent of pine needles.

'You are the most stressful person I have ever met,' he whispered into her jacket.

She hugged him back. Her hands were still cold, gripping the fabric of his shirt tightly. 'I messed up,' she murmured. 'I saw a new fruiting body on a different elevation line and I got greedy. I thought I could make it back before the fog rolled in.'

'You survived,' Ashish said, pulling back to look at her face. There was a scratch on her cheek, and she looked exhausted, but she was entirely, undeniably alive.

'Dev told me you gave them my GPS coordinates,' she said. 'From my Garmin sync.'

'It was a partial sync. A fluke of a 2G signal.'

'It's the only reason they searched that specific ravine,' she said softly. 'They were concentrating on the main slope until they got that data point. You debugged my location, Ashish.'

'I am never letting you go to a place without Wifi again.'

She gave a weak, tired laugh. 'I'm a field botanist. That's a very restrictive mandate.'

'We'll compromise,' he said. 'I'll buy you a satellite phone.'

'You're absurd.'

'And you are alive. And safe.'

Chapter 26: The Recovery

Ashish stayed in Nainital for five days.

He worked remotely from her hostel's common room, sitting with his laptop while Prachi sat on the couch with her elevated ankle, updating her field notes and cataloguing the photographs she had taken before the fall.

'Was it worth it?' Ashish asked on the third day, pointing at her laptop screen, where a starkly clear image of the bracket fungus showed the exact color banding she had been looking for.

Prachi looked at the picture. Then she looked at the heavy cast on her ankle.

'Scientifically? Yes,' she said. 'Personally? I spent eight hours under a freezing rock thinking about how I hadn't replied to your last text about Gerald the pothos. That was a very specific kind of regret.'

Ashish paused his typing. 'That was your primary regret?'

'I'm a responsible plant aunt,' Prachi said defensively.

'You almost died on a mountain and you were worried about leaving me unsupervised with a houseplant.'

'Well, given your track record with watering—'

Ashish crossed the room, sat on the edge of the couch next to her injured leg, and looked at her extremely seriously.

'Prachi,' he said. 'I will buy a moisture meter. I will automate a hydroponic drip system. I will never overwater a plant for the rest of my natural life. But you have to promise me you won't vanish into a fog bank again.'

She looked at him, the banter dropping away, leaving only the quiet, genuine weight of what had happened.

'I promise,' she said. 'I'll be careful.'

He took her hand. It was finally warm.

Chapter 27: The Data Doesn't Lie

On his last day in Nainital before heading back to Bangalore, Prachi formally finished her research paper.

She clicked 'Save', exported it as a PDF, and looked at Ashish.

'Do you want to read it?'

'I won't understand ninety percent of the vocabulary.'

'Read the conclusion.'

Ashish took her laptop. He scrolled past pages of morphological analysis, spore measurements, and ecological distribution charts, stopping at the final summary paragraph.

...therefore, the distinct pore morphology and altitudinal variance confirm this as a novel species within the Trametes genus. Taking into account its unique characteristics, the proposed nomenclature is Trametes prachii.

He smiled. 'You did it.'

He scrolled down slightly further.

Acknowledgments:

This research was supported by the Kumaon University Department of Botany. The author extends special thanks to Devendra Singh for field assistance, and to Ashish , whose expertise in telemetry and systems analysis proved unexpectedly vital to the completion of this field work. Sometimes, the most important network connections are the ones we do not plan for.

Ashish stared at the screen for a long time.

Then he looked up at Prachi, who was suddenly finding the texture of her hostel blanket extremely interesting, a slight flush creeping up her neck.

'Unexpectedly vital,' Ashish quoted softly.

'It's academic language. It's supposed to be understated.'

'I like it,' he said. 'It's accurate.'

He leaned forward and kissed her. It was their first kiss — careful because of her ankle, quiet because of the hostel common room, and absolutely, perfectly vital.

Chapter 28: March (Again)

When he returned to Bangalore, the distance felt different.

It wasn't an obstacle anymore; it was just a logistical variable. They had survived an HR security thriller, a snowstorm, and a literal search-and-rescue operation. Eighteen hundred kilometers was merely geography.

Because Prachi was restricted to bed rest for three weeks, their evening calls grew even longer. They watched movies together over video calls. He helped her compile her presentation slides for the upcoming conference in Pune.
And Gerald the pothos, bathed in the glow of Ashish's new, meticulously calibrated grow-light system, continued to thrive with the arrogant success of a plant that knew it was deeply loved.

Chapter 29: Almost

He didn't say it.

Not on the rhododendron path. Not at dinner that evening, at a small restaurant near Mallital where she ordered sternly and confidently and he deferred to her judgment because he was out of his element and she was absolutely in hers. Not on the walk back to his guesthouse, when the lake reflected the full moon so precisely that the sky and water became indistinguishable and she stopped to look at it and he stopped because she had stopped.

He said, instead: 'Tell me something you haven't told me yet.'

She looked at the lake. 'I'm afraid of not being taken seriously,' she said. 'The fungus work. What if I'm wrong? What if it's been classified somewhere and I missed it and it's embarrassing?'

'It hasn't been. You'd have found it.'

'I think I'm right,' she said. 'But thinking you're right isn't the same as people believing you're right.'

'You'll have the data,' he said. 'You already have most of it. The data will do the believing for you.'

She looked at him. 'You sound like a scientist.'

'I work with systems,' he said. 'Systems don't lie if you read them right.'

A pause. The moon on the water.

'Your turn,' she said. 'Something you haven't told me.'

And this — this was the moment. He could feel it, the way you feel the top of a slope before you commit to going down.

'I came here,' he said slowly, 'because I wanted to see what you look like in the places you describe. And the places are what I expected. And you're not what I expected — you're better and more immediate than a phone can hold.' He paused. 'That's all.'

She looked at him for a long moment. Long enough that he started composing a follow-up sentence as a retreat strategy.

'Okay,' she said. Very quietly. Like a door opening.

Chapter 30: A New Complication

The crisis arrived, as crises always did in Nainital, from the weather.

On the fifth morning of his week, the clouds came back — low and thick and laden. By 11 AM, the temperature had dropped three degrees and the first snow had begun to fall. Not the charming, picturesque snow of January postcards but the businesslike, accumulating snow of a genuine hill-station weather event.

By noon, the road to Delhi had been temporarily shut due to conditions on the descent. Ashish's return bus was cancelled.

He called Prachi from his guesthouse, where the proprietor had brought him an extra blanket and a pointed look that said: I told you this could happen but I knew better than to tell you and you have exactly the look of a person from the plains in February.

'My bus is cancelled,' he told Prachi.

'I know,' she said. She didn't sound displeased. 'The road will be clear by tomorrow evening, probably. Maybe day after.'

'I have to be back at the office on Monday.'

'It's Thursday. You have time.' A pause. 'Also, Ashish. You're snowed in in Nainital. That's not a problem. That's a story you'll tell for years.'

'My laptop is about to die and I don't have the right charger.'

'That,' she said, 'is a problem. Give me twenty minutes.'

She arrived at his guesthouse at 1 PM with a borrowed charger, a tiffin box of her dhaba uncle's hot dal-rice, and a completely composed expression that suggested she had been planning for this exact contingency.

He stared at the tiffin box. 'You brought food.'

'You looked like you were about to make terrible decisions about food. I preempted it.'

'The weather trapped me and you brought me lunch.'

'Yes. Eat. Your hands are cold.'

He sat at the small table in the guesthouse room with its view of the lake now blanketed in falling white, and ate the best dal of his life while she sat across from him and told him about the time a similar snowfall had trapped her entire field research group on the slope for six hours, during which her professor had taught them card games and one particularly anxious student had tried to call her mother every fifteen minutes until the network dropped and she'd had to be physically given hot chocolate and made to breathe.

'Were you the anxious student?' Ashish asked.

'I was the one administering the hot chocolate,' Prachi said. 'I don't do badly with situations. I do badly with uncertainty.'

'What's the difference?'

She thought about it. 'A situation is something happening. Uncertainty is not knowing what's going to happen. The first one I can act in. The second one I just have to wait through.'

A pause. The snow fell softly outside. The room was very warm from the guesthouse's old electric heater humming in the corner.

'Me too,' Ashish said. 'On the uncertainty part.'

She looked at him. He looked at her. The snow outside was very focused on its own business.

Chapter 31: The Thing Said

He told her on the evening of the second snow day, when the lake had turned white and the town lights refracted through the ice and the guesthouse proprietor had given them both chai without being asked because he was old and had seen many things and knew when two people needed a warm drink and a quiet room.

They were sitting at the window, watching the snow, and Ashish had been working up to it all day in the way that engineers work up to things they're not sure about — by running internal simulations and none of the outcomes being reassuring and eventually deciding that shipping is better than perpetual beta.

'Prachi,' he said. 'I'm going to say something and I'd like you to let me finish before you say anything, because I've been practicing.'

'You've been practicing?'

'In the bathroom. Yes.'

She pressed her lips together, clearly fighting very hard.

'Don't laugh,' he said.

'I'm not laughing.'

'You're about to.'

'Tell me the thing, Ashish.'

He looked at the lake. 'I messaged you by mistake in November. Completely wrong number. And I should have corrected it immediately and I didn't, and I think the reason I didn't is that something in the first reply — that sounds very stressful, are you okay — was exactly what I needed someone to say to me and I hadn't realized I needed it. And I've been talking to you for five months and I came here and the mountains are what you described and you are what I expected and more than I expected and I don't know what to do about that except tell you, because I am extremely bad at uncertainty and I would rather know than not know.' He stopped. 'That's the thing. That's all.'

Prachi was quiet. Outside, snow fell on the frozen lake.

'Ashish,' she said.

'Yes.'

'You rehearsed that.'

'I said I practiced. Yes.'

'In the bathroom.'

'Correct.'

A pause. Then: 'It was very good.'

'Thank you—'

'And I've been waiting for you to say it since the rhododendron walk,' she added. 'So you took about four days longer than necessary.'

He looked at her. She was looking at the lake, the corner of her mouth tilting up, and the snow was making everything soft and unhurried.

'Four days,' he said.

'Minimum.'

'I was iterating.'

'You were procrastinating.'

'In software, we call it—'

'I know what you call it in software.' She turned to look at him. 'I'm a botanist. We call it growth at the appropriate pace. Which is more generous.' She held his gaze. 'Same answer either way.'

Chapter 32: The Distance, Renegotiated

He went back to Bangalore on a Sunday, two days later than planned, on a road still slightly dramatic from the snow but passable. She walked with him to the bus stand at the same hour she had met him — 6 AM, same chill in the air, different quality to the silence between them.

He had Gerald's first leaf photo saved on his phone. He had five months of their conversation, scrollable proof of a thing built incrementally until it was so large and solid you couldn't see how you'd assembled it.

'This is the part where I point out that Bangalore and Nainital are not close,' Prachi said, at the bus stand.

'Eighteen hundred kilometers, roughly,' Ashish said. 'I Googled.'

'I know. I Googled too.'

A pause. A bus somewhere revving its engine with great morning conviction.

'The fungus paper,' he said. 'When you write it up — where do you submit it?'

'There's a mycology journal in Pune. And one international one.' She looked at her boots. 'If it's accepted, I might present in April. In Pune.'

'Pune is close to Bangalore,' Ashish said carefully.

'Three hours by bus,' Prachi said.

'Four and a half, more accurately. I checked.'

'You also checked that.'

'I check things,' he said. 'It's my job.'

She finally looked up. The same look she had given him on the rhododendron path — too much in it to be casual, too controlled to be careless.

'Come to Pune,' she said. 'If I present. Come and watch me present a fungus.'

'There is nothing I would rather do,' Ashish said. And he meant it entirely.

Chapter 33: Gerald's Update

He got back to Bangalore on Sunday night. The apartment was warm and familiar and smelled of the city he'd lived in for three years. Gerald sat on the desk, lush and trailing now, four new leaves open and reaching.

Ashish stood in front of him and said: 'She's real. It's real. Everything's real.'

Gerald did what pothos plants do, being plants: nothing, and therefore everything.

He messaged Prachi: Home. Gerald says hi.

Her reply came two minutes later: Gerald is better behaved than you. He hasn't missed a watering in weeks.

I've grown as a person.

You've grown as a plant parent. Somewhat different.

How's the fungus?

Getting there. April is looking real. I'll know by March if I'm presenting.

Tell me the moment you know.

I will. Get some sleep. You have a staging environment to fix probably.

Definitely. Goodnight, Prachi.

Goodnight, Ashish.

He set his phone down. He looked at his apartment — still mostly dark, still minimal — and thought that it would need more plants. He thought that Gerald looked particularly pleased with himself, small green leaves catching the lamplight.

'Don't be smug,' he told Gerald.

Gerald, to his credit, was not smug. He was only growing.

Chapter 34: March — The Confirmation

The call came on a Tuesday in March at 9:43 PM — Prachi's time, Bangalore's time, the same time as always.

He picked up expecting the usual — dhaba update, fungus status, Gerald enquiry, various complaints about lab partners. Instead:

'Ashish.'

Something in her voice.

'Yes?'

'The paper was accepted for presentation. April 18th. Pune.'

He leaned back in his chair. Gerald's newest leaf caught the lamplight. The Bangalore fan spun above. The world continued in its usual business.

'Pune,' he said.

'Pune,' she confirmed.

'I'm booking a ticket tonight.'

A pause. Then, through the phone, from a hostel room in Nainital with notoriously inconsistent hot water, he heard Prachi Joshi — botanist, fungus-classifier, keeper of mountains, person who had answered a wrong number in November and somehow become the most important call he made every day — exhale with the relief of someone who has been right all along, about everything, and is only now beginning to let themselves believe it.

'Book the return for Monday,' she said. 'I have the whole weekend after the conference. I found a botanical garden near the city that has zero tourists in April and an extremely interesting lichen collection.'

'I don't know anything about lichen,' he said.

'I'll teach you,' she said. 'It's what I do.'

Chapter 35: The Botanical Sanctuary

They reached the botanical garden in Pune on Sunday afternoon. It was early April, the heat of the plains heavy, but inside the canopy of the sanctuary, it was green, quiet, and smelling of damp, rich soil—Prachi's element. She walked slower today, her ankle fully recovered but her high academic armor entirely down.

They sat on a weathered stone bench beneath a massive Ficus tree. Prachi traced the thick veins of a leaf with her index finger, her gaze unusually soft and unanchored from her notebooks.

She turned to look at Ashish. 'I tell you stories so you don't notice I am scared,' she said quietly, her voice operating at a low, vulnerable frequency that held none of her usual academic distance. 'I am the topper, the strong one holding up the slides. I fix the labs. But when I was on that fog-bound slope under the rock, I didn't care about the research. I only cared that I hadn't sent a reply back to your absolute midnight message.'

'I want you with me, Ashish. I need you to notice when I am not okay, because I am terribly bad at asking for it.'

Ashish looked at her. He didn't pull back. He reached up to cup her face with steady, deliberate care, his dark eyes locked entirely on hers, recognizing the profound weight of her vulnerability. 'Prachi,' he said. 'You don't have to prove anything to the hills or the slides anymore. You have held my darkness alive for months. I love your strength, but I adore your vulnerability. Every note I write isn't a check-up; it's a statement that you are the absolute center of my world.'

He watched her make that small, happy head-shake movement against his palm, a tear finally giving way into absolute relief, and the intense density of belonging filled the quiet of the Pune sanctuary as they stood together, and somewhere inside a dark room in Bangalore, Gerald the Pothos unfurled a new, bright leaf on his desk—entirely sure of the light.

— The End —
